content.problemStatement:
There is a slot machine with NNN reels. Each reel has characters aaa to zzz **in order**.

The initial configuration of the machine is given by a string SSS of length NNN, i.e., initially, the ithi^{th}ith reel from the left reads the character SiS_iSi​.

You can perform the following types of operations on the slot machine:


Type 111: Pick a reel iii and rotate it upwards. For example, if SiS_iSi​ reads a\texttt{a}a, it becomes b\texttt{b}b, b\texttt{b}b becomes c\texttt{c}c and so on (z\texttt{z}z becomes a\texttt{a}a).
Type 222: Pick a reel iii and rotate it downwards. For example, if SiS_iSi​ reads b\texttt{b}b, it becomes a\texttt{a}a, c\texttt{c}c becomes b\texttt{b}b and so on (a\texttt{a}a becomes z\texttt{z}z).
You are allowed to perform the type 111 operation **at most** PPP times and the type 222 operation **at most** QQQ times.

Find the lexicographically **minimum** possible configuration of the slot machine that can be obtained by performing the above operations optimally.

Note that string XXX is lexicographically smaller than string YYY, if Xi<YiX_i \lt Y_iXi​<Yi​, where iii is the first index where XXX and YYY differ.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains TTT, the number of test cases. The description of each test case follows.
The first line of each test case contains three integers NNN, P,P,P, and QQQ, the number of reels in the slot machine, the maximum number of type 111 operations allowed and the maximum number of type 222 operations allowed.
The second line of each test case contains SSS, the initial configuration of the slot machine.


Output Format
For each test case, print the lexicographically **minimum** possible configuration of the slot machine that can be obtained by performing the above operations.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains three integers N, P and Q — the number of reels, the maximum number of upward rotations and the maximum number of downward rotations respectively. The second line contains a string S of length N consisting of lowercase English letters, the initial configuration.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the lexicographically minimum string that can be obtained after performing at most P type‑1 operations and at most Q type‑2 operations.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each test case the algorithm greedily picks the smallest reachable character at every position while respecting the remaining budgets, reproducing the results shown in the statement.

First case no operations allowed, string stays "z". Second case one upward rotation turns "z" into "a". Third case: first reel "b" is turned down to "a" using the single allowed type‑2 operation, the second reel stays "a".

In the first test case three upward rotations turn the first three "z" into "a"; the last "z" cannot be changed. In the second test case the first "c" is moved down two steps to "a" using the two type‑2 operations, leaving no more type‑2 budget; the remaining characters cannot be improved.

solution.approach:
Process the reels from left to right. For the current character c (0‑based index in the alphabet) try target letters t = 'a' … 'z' in increasing order. For each t compute:
  upDist = (t - c + 26) % 26   // number of type‑1 operations needed
  downDist = (c - t + 26) % 26 // number of type‑2 operations needed
If upDist ≤ remainingP we can obtain t by using upDist upward rotations; if downDist ≤ remainingQ we can obtain t by using downDist downward rotations. Choose the first t for which at least one of the two conditions holds – this is the smallest reachable character for the current position. When both directions are possible, use the one with the smaller cost (if equal, any direction works). Decrease the corresponding budget (P or Q) and continue to the next position. Because earlier positions dominate the lexicographic order, this greedy choice is optimal: any sacrifice of a budget to keep a later character smaller can never improve the already fixed earlier character.
The algorithm runs in O(N·26) ≈ O(N) time and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N·26) per test case, i.e. linear in the total length of all strings.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    it = iter(data)
    t = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(t):
        n = int(next(it)); P = int(next(it)); Q = int(next(it))
        s = list(next(it).strip())
        for i in range(n):
            c = ord(s[i]) - 97
            for t_char in range(26):
                up = (t_char - c) % 26
                down = (c - t_char) % 26
                if up <= P:
                    s[i] = chr(97 + t_char)
                    P -= up
                    break
                if down <= Q:
                    s[i] = chr(97 + t_char)
                    Q -= down
                    break
        out_lines.append(''.join(s))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
